Brainstorm the FULL Product spec, before sending to both opus/codex use an opus agent to ask me questions for high-level user guidance gathering to understand the product further. Use 5 opus agents in parallel to come up with questions.

The high-level of the product we're building:

Olas is a private-first instagram for small communities (like families or closed groups). Uses NIP-29 and perhaps MLS later (for now we only build the NIP-29 part). Flow typically will be people download the app to invite others or are invited into someone's app. The main feed is a feed of all posts from people in all the groups the user belongs to. A per-group view is necessary. Media is either images or videos using the proper NIPs for images or videos. Comments are NIP-22 comments. The UX must be highly cared for, specially during invite but throughout the product needs to be highly polished. We need market research performed online to research how people express their interest in a product like this and what features they want as well as competitive market landscape; without any mention of “nostr” we need to identify a way of differentiating the app.

Synthesized 30 questions from 5 parallel Opus agents into 6 categories: who it's really for, the invite/onboarding moment, what "private" actually promises, community lifecycle, feed/posting/engagement, and competitive positioning.

Ok, let's pivot for now: let's just build a good pictures-feed-first client (which is what Olas has been from the beginning in it's previous incarnations) we'll add NIP-29 stuff later.

Strong focus on WOT filtering (NMP provides WOT filtering; if something is inadequate or incomplete don't hack around it: file a gh issue on the NMP repo, then launch a PERT flow on the NMP codebase in this computer, land the PR and consume it on the Olas codebase: part of building this app is improving NMP itself and finding gaps — gaps MUST BE skeptically validated by codex: we want to avoid bloating the concerns of NMP and protecting the boundaries of NMP from single-app concerns it's important)

Create a full product spec of everything the app should have, user flows, etc.

While the mockup is getting built with the onboarding; start PETR the actual build out of the actual app for iOS and android; both in parallel, both completely feature complete; I expect this task to take hours to complete because the entire thing is fully implemented in a very long full plan of workflow of teams
Launched full PERT workflow for iOS + Android build across 5 phases: Plan (3 parallel Opus agents), Rust Core, iOS + Android in parallel, Review, and Test.
Ensure that NMP architecture is CLOSELY followed; no hacks

Stopped and rewrote the workflow — removed all local reimplementations of WoT scoring, Blossom HTTP, keypair generation, and event polling, replacing them with proper nmp-ffi, nmp-blossom, nmp-wot, and nmp-defaults calls. Review phase now fails on any boundary violation.

A session-scoped Stop hook is now active with condition: "ensure the whole app, both ios and android are working, tested (via haiku agents actually using the app) -- fully polished (use opus agents that review screenshots of the app for polish feedback, pixel-perfect kind of stuff), complete working features with NO stubs, mocks, fake data, using real relays, real keys, publishing actual photos, editing the photos with cool filters and everything else that is expected from an instagram-like app.". Briefly acknowledge the goal, then immediately start (or continue) working toward it — treat the condition itself as your directive and do not pause to ask the user what to do. The hook will block stopping until the condition is met. It auto-clears once the condition is met — do not tell the user to run `/goal clear` after success; that's only for clearing a goal early.
Rust layer verified clean (no local reimplementations), build workflow running, polish-test workflow staged for post-build verification with Haiku testing and Opus pixel-perfect review.
